author: @ckimsey77
text:
It's really nice to see someone explain in detail actually how something works, and most excellent to see you using equations and differentials rather than just showing a "real working circuit" demo with o theory to back up why it works. Too many people just copy circuits and make videos as if they engineered it personally.  Ima masters in chem. engr. so I know the math, actually love it so that part is not boring for someone seeking understanding rather than copying.

I only had basic knowledge of electronics, but I am very capable of the calculus need for time variance differentials with inductance/capacitance.  Applying it to building circuits I lacked, but have self taught to a moderate level; this video was really helpful for me to further my underdtanding.  

My ultimate goal is trying to persue an idea ive had for some time to make hydrolysis more efficient in theory by using a high frequency ac signal instead of dc to create hydrogen gas using low voltage driving sources.  The amount of gas generated is proportional to current used as power causing electron transfer redox reactions.  However, I was thinking maybe like a microwave heats water by exciting water molecules using a wave with similar size amplitude as the width of the very polar H2O molecule it adds rotational energy, which in turn raises the temp (T is just total average energy of system rotational, translation/velocity and vibrational)  I think the right frequency could effectively increase the vibrational excitement of the bonds, ultimately making them weaker and easier to break.  With a strong field between electrodes very close together to minimize cell resistance I believe the right frequency could "vibrate" some of the molecules apart breaking bonds due to mechanical strain caused by the field oscillation.  

I have been limited to trying this theory to the matter of building the proper circuit to provide the signalI believe is required, and this could be a way to achieve that.  Is there a significant votage drop when a load is applied to output?  Does the load affect the frequency?  My cell will have many plates close together to minimize resistance and a rate slow enough the gas generation does not significantly reduce surface interactions.  If this worked, you would get extra gas generated with the same applied power, the normal amount converted by current PLUS more simply from frequency destruction breaking bonds to create free radicals to form H2 and O2 as well.  Do you think that could work???

author: @0rchimaru
text: Hi, just a basic question. I am a little confuse with the purpose of the BJT in the circuit. Am I right to say that it is only needed because to supply a +5V directly from the MCU to the MOSFET, it is not sufficient to power it completely as mentioned in the video. So with the BJT we are able to switch between +12V to power the Mosfet on and off through the adjusted switching frequency provided by the arduino. Lastly, is the arduino utilising PWM here?

replies.author: @douggale5962
replies.text: The BJT allows the digital signal from the Arduino to turn the MOSFET off and on regardless of the boost converter supply voltage. The 10k resistor is a pull-up resistor, it pulls the gate of the MOSFET high when the BJT is off. When current is driven into the base of the BJT by the Arduino, the BJT conducts and pulls the gate of the MOSFET low. This would work for a wide range of boost converter supply voltages. Think of the BJT like the bottom resistor of a voltage divider, when the bottom resistor is low value (a conducting BJT in this analogy), the voltage between the two hypothetical resistors is very close to zero. When the bottom resistor is a very high value (a switched-off BJT in this analogy) the voltage between the two resistors is close to the boost converter supply voltage.

replies.author: @joemwangi5813
replies.text: Yes you're right BJT is used because 5 v is so less to switch mosfet and Arduino provides pwm signal to the switching

author: @tincugabriel8320
text: Hey man, great videos. I only have one question that's been bugging me for some time. Everyone says that when the switch is open and the current has to travel through the diode, the magnetic field inside the capacitor collapses. Is there any clear explanation lying around for that? I mean the first circuit is now open, but the second one is closed, so current keeps flowing through the inductor. Secondly, what makes the topology of the boost converted step up voltage, while the topology of the buck converter step it down? The only big difference in the circuits i saw is basically the diode placement. Thanks.

replies.author: @supershaye
replies.text:
Tincu Gabriel 
First question: why does magnetic field around inductor collapse when switch open?
This is because the voltage across the inductor is (Vin - Vout - 0.7). As this is a boost converter, Vout is bigger than Vin so the result of that expression is negative. The inductor equation is V = L(di/dt). V is the voltage across the inductor which is negative. Therefore, di/dt = -V/L which tells you that the current is decreasing by V/L every second. When the current is decreasing, the magnetic field collapses because the magnetic field strength is proportional to the current.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text:
zuluknob you should aim to match the  the inductance as closely as possible. You can buy meters that measure inductance reasonably cheaply, so you could use one of those to measure the inductance of the existing coil.

I suppose the crudest approach would to do this and determine the inductance and then wind a new one with slightly thicker wire to prevent burn out.

I suppose this might work, but there are other features of the inductor you would need to match like the saturation current which will be determined by the thickness of the wire, the core material, any air gaps etc.

Also the inductor will have a self-resonant frequency since it has distributed capacitance, this might be important in the circuit depending on how it is designed.

If it's burned out you don't have a lot to lose, but you can see the conclusion is that you should aim to use something as close as possible to the original. Of course you do this at your own risk :D
